coredata.dc:title: Graphene-Quantum-Dots-induced facile growth of porous molybdenum doped Ni3S2 nanoflakes as efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiefei
Zhang, Xueqiong
Zhang, Zehao
Li, Zhenyu
Gao, Mingming
Wei, Hang
Chu, Haibin
coredata.dc:description: Taking into account the development of carbon-neutral economy, exploring noble-metal-free electrocatalysts is still a great challenge for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) through water splitting. Herein, Graphene-Quantum-Dots (GQDs) loaded and molybdenum doped porous Ni3S2 hybrid (G-Mo-Ni3S2) was in situ grown on Ni foam by a GQDs induced hydrothermal method and directly used both as a HER and OER electrocatalyst. The in situ coupling of GQDs and molybdenum doping Ni3S2 not only allow the modulation of electrochemical behavior, but also enhance electrical conductivity and expose more active sites. Benefiting from the inductive effect of GQDs, the in situ formed G-Mo-Ni3S2 porous structure showed outstanding HER performance with a low overpotential of 68 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and OER property with overpotential of 326 mV at 20 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, respectively. In addition, a two-electrode cell using G-Mo-Ni3S2 is constructed to deliver low cell voltage of 1.58 V at water-splitting current of 10 mA cm−2 with remarkable stability for more than 50 h. This work serves as a promising strategy for the construction of efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Induced
Mo-doped
Ni3S2
Electrocatalysis
Overall water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of G-Mo-Ni3S2 on pretreated nickel foam

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) Mo-Ni3S2/NF, (b) G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and (c) G-Mo-Ni3S2-5/NF. (d) XRD patterns of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF.

TEM images of (a) GQDs with HRTEM image as inset, (b) G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 and (c–f) corresponding elemental mapping of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2. (g, h) HRTEM images of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2, (i) FT-IR spectrums of GQDs and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2.
XPS profiles of (a) survey (b) Ni 2p, (c) S 2p and (d) Mo3d of Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF.

HER performance of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and Pt/C catalysts (with iR corrections) in 1M KOH electrolytes. (a) Polarization curves of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and Pt/C with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. (b) Comparison of the catalyst overpotentials at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. (c) Tafel plots of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and Pt/C. (d) The double layer capacitances (Cdl) of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF. (e) Nyquist plots of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF. (f) Chronopotentiometric measurements of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF.

OER performance of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and RuO2 catalysts (with iR corrections) in 1M KOH electrolytes. (a) Polarization curves of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and RuO2 with a scan rate of 5 mV/s (b) Comparison of the catalyst overpotentials at a current density of 20 mA cm−2. (c) Tafel plots of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF and RuO2. (d) Chronopotentiometric measurements of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF.
(a) Polarization curves and (b) long-term durability tests at 1.65 V of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF for overall water splitting in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte.
The synthesis processes of Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF.

head.ce:title: Graphene-Quantum-Dots-induced facile growth of porous molybdenum doped Ni3S2 nanoflakes as efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia Engineering and Technology Research Center for Catalytic Conversion and Utilization of Carbon Resource Molecules, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot, 010021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Inner Mongolia Engineering and Technology Research Center for Catalytic Conversion and Utilization of Carbon Resource Molecules

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Taking into account the development of carbon-neutral economy, exploring noble-metal-free electrocatalysts is still a great challenge for both hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) through water splitting. Herein, Graphene-Quantum-Dots (GQDs) loaded and molybdenum doped porous Ni3S2 hybrid (G-Mo-Ni3S2) was in situ grown on Ni foam by a GQDs induced hydrothermal method and directly used both as a HER and OER electrocatalyst. The in situ coupling of GQDs and molybdenum doping Ni3S2 not only allow the modulation of electrochemical behavior, but also enhance electrical conductivity and expose more active sites. Benefiting from the inductive effect of GQDs, the in situ formed G-Mo-Ni3S2 porous structure showed outstanding HER performance with a low overpotential of 68 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and OER property with overpotential of 326 mV at 20 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, respectively. In addition, a two-electrode cell using G-Mo-Ni3S2 is constructed to deliver low cell voltage of 1.58 V at water-splitting current of 10 mA cm−2 with remarkable stability for more than 50 h. This work serves as a promising strategy for the construction of efficient bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting.

ce:section.ce:para: With ever-increasing utilization of fossil fuels and growing environmental impact, sustainable and renewable energy have been considerable expected [1,2]. Electrochemical water splitting has garnered growing attention as one of the most promising approaches to supply green and renewable hydrogen fuel, which requires a theoretically splitting potential of 1.23 V [3–6]. Nevertheless, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) as essential reactions of electrochemical water splitting are severely hampered by the large overpotential, causing the electrolysis efficiency is not very satisfactory [4–8]. So far, though noble metal (Pt, Ru, etc.) [9,10] or their alloy (AuPd, etc.) [11] show efficient electrocatalytic activity for the HER and OER, their low abundance and high cost hinders the wide applications. In this respect, well-designed noble-metal-free electrode materials with different active sites have been considered to be promising electrocatalysts which have higher activity for HER or OER [12–15]. Especially, considering cathode and anode catalysts should work simultaneously on a single water-splitting device, the compatibility of two electrodes are highly needed [16–20]. Therefore, much attention should be paid to develop effective strategies that explore bifunctional active sites in a single electrocatalyst in the same electrolyte for overall water splitting.

Recently, transition metal oxides [11–25], sulfides [26–31], carbides [31–33], phosphates [34–36] have been applied for the water splitting reaction. Among these catalysts, transition metal sulfides has been intensively investigated as a prospective catalyst due to its low cost, stability in wide pH range, and most importantly high activity [37–40]. Particularly, nickel sulfide has been extensively studied due to its highly active for OER, but the overall water splitting capacity are restricted because of the HER is not up to the mark. In order to enhance the catalytic activity for the overall water splitting, nickel sulfide are usually hybridized with other efficient electrocatalyst with higher HER activity, incorporated with metal dopings or introduced defects [41,42]. Indeed, these heterogeneous nanostructures or heterogeneous metal atoms can induce heterointerfaces and result in varied active sites. Moreover, a large amount of research has focused on the direct growth of nanostructured catalysts on conducting substrates [43–45], such as carbon cloth, nickel foam, metal foil, and so forth, which can indirectly improve the poor electrical transport behavior and low kinetic process. However, this strategy can only solve part of the conductive problems, because the conducting substrates cannot improve the internal conductivity of nanostructured catalysts. Therefore, various forms of nanocarbon materials can be used as an internal regulation which coupled with electrocatalysts to boost the whole catalytic performance.

As a member of the carbon materials family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) play a very unique role in material synthesis and can improve catalytic activity due to their many natural merits [10,46,47]. Firstly, GQDs with a 0D planar structure can alter the material morphology for constructing various nanomaterials. Secondly, when GQDs assemble with other active component, there will be more interspaces and defects which can improve electrocatalytic activity in the hybrids, besides more pathways for both ions and electrons transfer are also obtained. More interestingly, the specific surface areas of GQDs is higher than other carbon materials since their diameters are just below 2 nm. Meanwhile, more organic groups can be anchored on the GQDs which benefits for the surface wettability in the water splitting reactions. In addition, the GQDs can also be doped by many other heteroatoms, such as N, P, S and so on, which further improve the conductivity and electrocatalytic properties. Considering the preparation process of GQDs is facile, GQDs provide new opportunities to design novel catalysts for HER and OER owing to their all these merits.

In this case, simultaneous incorporation of nanocarbon and metal doping in nickel sulfide may be more meaningful in altering the electronic structure and introducing defects, which further improve catalytic performance. Herein, GQDs loaded and molybdenum doped Ni3S2 nanosheets (G-Mo-Ni3S2) with different GQDs contents were successfully prepared via a facile one-step GQDs induced hydrothermal method. The experimental results demonstrate that pure molybdenum doped Ni3S2 hybrids without GQDs exhibited morphology of large particles with aggregation and showed negligible HER and OER activities in alkaline media. However, the obtained G-Mo-Ni3S2 with proper GQDs loading amount had vertically aligned nanoplates supported on Ni foam due to the inducing effect. Which showed both superior HER performance with a low overpotential of 68 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and OER property with overpotential of 326 mV at 20 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, respectively. Such hybrid nanostructures and lattice distortion increase the exposure of different active sites, and the stronger electronic interaction between the metal ions would be beneficial to the catalytic activity. More significantly, the GQDs loading and their in-situ growth on the conducting NF provide efficient electrical transport behavior and gas release pathway. In addition, this superior abilities toward overall water splitting of G-Mo-Ni3S2 with proper GQDs doping amount is featured by an excellent potential of 1.58 V (at 10 mA cm−2) and prominent stability for over 50 h, which makes it promising as a bifunctional catalyst to scale-up water electrolysis.

Ni foam (thickness of 1.0 mm) were purchased from Jiashide Foam Metal Co., Ltd., Suzhou. Acetone (≥99.9%), ethanol (≥99.9%), HCl (37%), were purchased from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd., Pt/C (20 wt %) and Nafion (5 wt %) were purchased from Johnson Metthey. Citric acid (≥99%), urea, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, CS(NH2)2 and RuO2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Nickel foam (3 cm × 4 cm × 0.1 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned with 3 M HCl solution for 30 min, subsequently, rinsed with acetone, ethanol and deionized water for three times to remove the nickel oxides on surface.

Firstly, citric acid 0.21 g and urea 0.18 g were added into 5 mL deionized water and fully stirred until they completely dissolved. Lastly, the obtained sample was transformed into 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept the autoclave at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling and dialysis, the GQDs were transferred to watch glass with heating by 80 °C until obtained the dry dish.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of G-Mo-Ni3S2 on pretreated nickel foam
ce:section.ce:para: The in-situ growth of Mo-Ni3S2 and G-Mo-Ni3S2 were successfully accomplished through the one-step hydrothermal reaction (180 °C, 12 h), in which pretreated Ni foam (3 cm × 4 cm) and a mixed (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (0.121 g) and CS(NH2)2 (0.038 g) solutions were used as precursors. From our experimental results, it is found that the adding amounts of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and CS(NH2)2 has little effect on the morphology and properties of the Mo-Ni3S2. In order to investigate the effect of GQDs amount on the morphology of Mo-Ni3S2, the adding amount of GQDs was 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 mg, respectively. The obtained products were denoted as G-Mo-Ni3S2-1, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2, G-Mo-Ni3S2-3, G-Mo-Ni3S2-4 and G-Mo-Ni3S2-5. The loading mass on NF was about 1.5 mg/cm2, which was in terms of the weight increment after catalyst preparation.

All the electrochemical measurements were performed on CHI 760 electrochemistry workstation. The working electrode was a Ni foam modified by catalyst (1 cm × 1 cm). SCE electrode and graphite rod were used as reference and counter electrode, respectively. The polarization curves with an iR compensation of at 85% were obtained using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) for HER and OER in 1.0 M KOH solution (pH = 14) at the scanning rate of 5 mV/s. The Tafel plots were derived from the OER and HER polarization curves (2 mV/s) and constructed by the Tafel equation. The chronopotentiometric (CP) measurements and LSV after continuous 1000 cycle cyclic voltammetry (CV) were also conducted to evaluate their durability. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were calculated in the frequency from 0.01 to 1000000 Hz in the 1M KOH. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) can be calculated through the electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) with cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots in a non- Faradaic region. So, we performed the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves with different scan rates (5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 mV/s) in the potential range from 0.05 to 0.22 V versus RHE to calculate Cdl. The plot of diverse scan rates versus the corresponding current density (j) between the anode and cathode (Δ j = j anodic - j cathodic)/2 at 0.135 V versus RHE can estimate the Cdl by their linear slope. The electrocatalytic performances for overall water splitting was measured by employing G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF as working electrodes for both OER and HER. The RuO2 and Pt/C catalysts were used for the baseline catalysts for all the electrochemical measurements. All the potentials exhibited in this paper were calculated by the Nernst equation:E
RHE = E
SCE + 0.242 V + 0.059 pH.

The Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on PANalytical Empyrean diffractometer operated at 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was operated on a THS-103X spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization was acquired on a FEI Tecnai F20 system carried out on an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The size and morphology of the adsorbents were observed by using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) (Hitachi, Model s-4800).

The crystalline GQDs was prepared using citric acid and urea as the precursor employing a hydrothermal treatment. TEM images are shown in the Fig. 2 a, which display that the GQDs are well dispersed and the diameter is below 5 nm. And the typical lattice fringe of GQDs (002) lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.330 nm can be observed the high-resolution TEM images (HRTEM) (Fig. 2a inset). To further reveal the detail microstructures of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2, typical TEM images (Fig.2b and Fig. S3) of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 powder scratched down from Ni foam substrate was obtained. It can be seen that the individual nanoflakes are continuous with disordered surface, and GQDs are homogeneously distributed in the Ni3S2 nanoplates (marked as yellow circles in Fig. S3). Moreover, the TEM-EDS and corresponding elemental mapping are conducted to analyze the composition of the G-Mo-Ni3S2-2. The results reveal the main composition and uniformly distribution of Ni, Mo, S and C elements (Fig. 2c–f). Meanwhile, the typical lattice fringe of GQDs (002) lattice fringes and Ni3S2 (110) lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.287 nm can be observed from the HRTEM images (Fig. 2g–h). Furthermore, Fig. 2i plots the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of GQDs and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2. For the GQDs, the stretching vibrations at 3412 cm−1 and 3342 cm−1 are assigned to N-H, and peaks at 1642 cm−1 and 1427 cm−1 are assigned to C-N functional groups, which indicates the existence of nitrogen element. Besides, 1524 cm−1 is assigned to the C-C bonds, which are absent in the bare Mo-Ni3S2 sample [10]. Interestingly, all of these characteristic peaks appear in the FTIR spectrum of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2, which suggests GQDs have been loaded into G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 sample. It is worth noting that the introduction of GQDs can induce distortions and dislocations in the Ni3S2 nanoplates, leading to the formation of defects. The organization of the well-ordered Ni3S2 nanoplates, uniformly distributed Mo element, and highly conductive GQDs result in the formation of mismatch and interfaces, which would lead to more active edge sites and higher electrocatalytic activity.

Fig. 3 a displays the XPS results of Mo-Ni3S2 and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 composites, which strongly support the surface of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 is composed of Ni, Mo, S, and C, which are agreement with its composition. Fig. 3b presents the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 level peaks around 855.63 and 874.01eV, respectively, as well as the corresponding satellite peaks on Mo-Ni3S2, indicating the formation of the Ni3S2 and is consistent with the previously reports [45]. However, the peaks of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 occur at binding energies of 855.35 and 873.75 eV. It declares that the electronic interactions due to the Mo doping and GQDs loading leads to redistribution of charge on the Ni3S2 surface. Additionally, peak fitting of the S 2p region of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 can be deconvoluted into four peaks (Fig. 3c). Binding energies of 161.73 eV and 164.05 eV corresponds to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 lines, respectively. It should be noted that the binding energy at 164.8 eV implies the formation of terminating S2− or unsaturated S atom, which are considered to be highly beneficial to the HER process [50]. In the Mo 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d), Mo-Ni3S2 and G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 exhibit well defined peaks at 230.4 and 231.6 eV, which can be assigned to 3d5/2 peaks of Mo4+ and Mo6+ ions [51,52].

To reveal the HER activity, Ni3S2/NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2/NF with different GQDs amounts were measured in a 1.0 M KOH solution. Thereinto, the NF were cut into 1 cm × 1 cm and directly utilized as working electrode. Their polarization curves are displayed in Fig. 4 a. Although Pt/C supported by NF offers the best performance, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF also exhibits excellent HER activity, which presents a smaller overpotential as low as 68 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. It is worth noting that the overpotential of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF is much smaller than those of NF only (η 10 = 227 mV), Mo-Ni3S2/NF (η 10 = 149 mV), and G-Mo-Ni3S2-5/NF (η 10 = 128 mV). The significant improvement in HER activity of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2 reflects the synergy of Mo and GQDs co-doping. The three-dimensional porous hierarchical nanostructures would improve the exposure of active edges, as indicated by the evolving HER activity associated with varied morphology. Obviously, the different GQDs loading in G-Mo-Ni3S2 had a great impact on the electro-catalytic performance. Different GQDs amount doped G-Mo-Ni3S2/NF were also tested in the same condition, and the results in Fig. S4a also indicate G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF obtains the best HER activity. Although other G-Mo-Ni3S2/NF catalysts perform larger overpotential than G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF, they still owe superior catalytic properties compared with Ni3S2/NF and Mo-Ni3S2/NF (Fig. 4b). This fully proves GQDs play a very important role in improving HER activity. Moreover, the HER properties of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF are comparable to the best noble-metal-free catalysts reported previously (Table S1).

Accordingly, Tafel slope can be acquired by the Tafel equation which is expressed through η = a + blogj. η represents the overpotential, and b is the Tafel slope reflecting the reaction mechanism and the kinetic properties of catalysts. Apparently, as shown in Fig. 4c, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF delivers a lower onset potential of 34 mV and a lower Tafel slope of 68 mV/dec, which are superior to NF (η onset = 199 mV, and b = 138 mV dec−1), Mo-Ni3S2/NF (η onset = 50 mV, and b = 121 mV/dec). The small Tafel slope of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF demonstrates the HER process is effectively facilitated on as prepared electrode and corresponds to the high hydrogen evolution rates at applied overpotentials. These results present that G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF has the lowest overpotential and the smallest Tafel slope among all the as-prepared samples except Pt/C/NF. These better activities are associated with its advanced morphology and structure by the addition of GQDs during the synthesis process. Appropriate GQDs amount alters the morphology of Mo-Ni3S2 from microspheres to three-dimensional porous hierarchical nanostructures. On one hand, the dense G-Mo-Ni3S2 nanoflakes are dominated by edges, which increase the density of active sites; on the other hand, the GQDs as carbon loading can improve the conductivity of the porous G-Mo-Ni3S2 nanoflakes. Moreover, above XPS analysis of G-Mo-Ni3S2 provides an obvious shift of the Ni 2p binding energy comparing with Mo-Ni3S2, this phenomenon illustrates that sufficient empty d orbitals can be obtained for enhancing binding with the H atom, which contributes to the obvious increasing of HER activity [31,50].

As we know, electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) and electron transfer rate of an electrocatalyst greatly affect its electrochemical activity. Therefore, rational measure of double-layer capacitances (Cdl) is analyzed, which is proportional to the ECSA and easy to comparison. As shown in Fig. S6, CV curves were obtained under different scan rate in 1.0 M KOH for all the as prepared catalysts. Fig. 4d indicates the Cdl on G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF are accordingly calculated to be 28 mF/cm2, which is much higher than those on Mo-Ni3S2/NF (22 mF/cm2) and NF (10 mF/cm2). The high Cdl value means the enriched active sites on G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF electrocatalyst. Meanwhile, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further performed. Fig. 4e displays the Nyquist plots for varied charge-transfer resistance (Rct) in G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF, and bare NF. The results are well consistent with the order of HER. Among these electrocatalysts, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF shows a lowest Rct value, which suggests the fastest faradaic process and rapidest electron transport for hydrogen evolution. Durability is another important parameter for evaluating HER catalyst. The long-term stability of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF was examined in 1.0 M KOH by continuous electrolysis under a constant overpotential of 100 mV. The obtained current density remains constant at ≈22 mA cm−2 over a testing period of 50 h (Fig. 4f) and their morphology and structure keeps no changing (Fig. S7), which confirms the satisfactory durability.

Mo-Ni3S2/NF and G-Mo-Ni3S2/NF with different GQDs amounts were also investigated for the OER performance in 1.0 M KOH, and Fig. 5 a shows the LSV curves for Mo-Ni3S2/NF, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF, bare NF and RuO2/NF at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Though compared with bare NF and Mo-Ni3S2/NF has slightly enhanced activity toward OER performance, Mo-Ni3S2/NF still presents an overpotential of 385 mV at 20 mA cm−2. However, G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF shows prominently improved OER activity with a lower η value of 326 mV at 20 mA cm−2. This value is parallel to commercial RuO2/NF (η 20 = 300 mV) and better than other G-Mo-Ni3S2/NF catalysts (Fig. 5b). Similar to the analysis in HER, the Cdl in OER process also can reflect the intrinsic activity of the as prepared electrocatalysts. Besides the higher density of active sites, the shift of Ni 2p from the XPS profiles of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF suggests that the enriched empty d orbitals strengthen the binding between Ni atom and OH* intermediate, and then the OER activity would be improved. In addition, the redox reaction of surface Ni species (II to III) also can generate OER active sites [50,53]. According to the Fig. 5a, the low overpotential (η 20 = 326 mV) of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF implies the OER activity is superior to other reported noble-metal-free electrocatalysts (Table S2). Fig. 5c elucidates the Tafel slope of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF is approximately 69 mV/dec, which is lower than Mo-Ni3S2/NF (203 mV/dec) and NF (247mV/dec). The η onset of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF is just 120 mV, outperforming Mo-Ni3S2/NF (301 mV) and NF (308 mV). To reveal the stability of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF for OER in alkaline medium, a long-term water oxidation is conducted at 340 mV (Fig. 5d), and the steady OER activity is observed for over 50 h.

In order to further declare the applicability of G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF as the bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting, an electrolyzer was constructed in which both cathode and anode were fabricated using the best-performed G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF (Fig. 6 a). Remarkably, the electrolyzer affords a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH at an applied potential of 1.58 V (vs RHE). Such activity is superior to those of NF, Mo-Ni3S2/NF and other bifunctional overall water splitting catalysts (Table S3), confirming that the G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF is efficient for overall water splitting. Moreover, the electrolyzer demonstrate a stable current density of ≈17 mA cm−2 for over 50 h with only minor degradation, when a cell voltage of only 1.65 V is needed (Fig. 6b). As shown in inset of Fig. 6b, the cathode/anode shows obvious H2 and O2 evolution which can be observed by unaided eyes. All above results confirms the satisfactory overall water splitting performance of our rationally designed G-Mo-Ni3S2-2/NF in the strong alkaline electrolyte.

In summary, GQDs loaded and molybdenum doped hierarchical Ni3S2/NF was successfully achieved via facile hydrothermal processes, utilizing GQDs to direct the growth of 3D porous hierarchical structure. In the G-Mo-Ni3S2/NF composite, the porous Ni3S2 nanoflakes are dominated by edges, which allow the well-exposed active sites. Such electrocatalyst shows a superior activity for HER performance with a low overpotential of 68 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and OER with overpotential of 326 mV at 20 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, respectively. When applied for overall water splitting, a cell potential of only 1.58 V is performed for the current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is better than some other reported electrocatalysts. Such efficient catalytic performance should be attributed to the modulation of electrical structure which favored chemisorption of intermediates and the increase of the active site density. Meanwhile, the facilitated electron transport results from both the internal GQDs loading and the external NF anchoring. Furthermore, considering its simplicity and low cost, this strategy may open up new routes for the design of other electrocatalysts with efficient water splitting properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21801140, 21561023, 51501094), the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region of China (21300-5183154), Program of Higher-level Talents of Inner Mongolia University (21300-5165155, 21300-5155104), “Grassland Talent” Program and “Grassland Talent” Innovation Team of Inner Mongolia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2019.03.023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Decoupled catalytic hydrogen evolution from a molecular metal oxide redox mediator in water splitting
Science
A large-scale renewable electricity supply system by 2030: solar, wind, energy efficiency, storage and inertia for the south west interconnected system (SWIS) in Western Australia
Renew. Energy
Perovskite oxide/carbon nanotube hybrid bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting
Electrochim. Acta

Chem. Soc. Rev.
